summary: McKenzie shares a day-in-the-life vignette from his dual existence as a Japanese salaryman and indie software entrepreneur. At his day job, he spent an hour helping a Japanese student with a school project about foreign countries, fielding questions about American schools. Meanwhile, his side business Bingo Card Creator continued generating daily sales, customer thank-you notes, and support requests without his active involvement. He marvels at reaching consistent daily sales and realizes the revenue is becoming significant enough to require professional tax and legal advice.
key_points: McKenzie's life in Japan as a foreigner creates unique cultural exchange moments that enrich his day job experience
His software business has reached the milestone of consistent daily sales (1-2 per day for five straight weekdays)
The back-of-envelope math ($24x30x12 > $8,000/year) shows his side project crossing from hobby into real taxable income
Customer emails arriving unsolicited (thank-you notes, referrals) signal organic word-of-mouth growth
money_quotes: This whole "get a sale every day" thing is starting to get a little overwhelming.
$24x30x12 > $8,000 per annum, and thats enough for, e.g., the government to start wondering "Hey, where is that all coming from".
I'll have to get legal before legal gets me.
I'm telling all my homeschooling friends!
I love both my jobs, actually.
key_insight: The moment a side project starts generating consistent daily revenue is the inflection point where a hobby becomes a real business requiring professional infrastructure.
